speaker: interviewer
text: It's September, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia. And camera  is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about your father, Edward Muller, and his involvement in the Jewish council.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his discussions about what was happening in the ghetto or outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember him saying that he didn't believe in the messiah, which I couldn't understand why everybody else did and he didn't. And the other thing was that he thought the only way would be to try and escape. And he tried to influence my-- his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, you know, you've got nothing to worry about. The messiah will come and rescue us. Why should we risk our lives when we we're alive? Because the penalty for escape was death. And my father said, there isn't such a thing as the messiah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that had to do with the fact that wasn't as religious as-- as the others, and they didn't have that sort of-- or maybe he was more realistic. Or maybe he was more courageous. I would like to think all those things, and I think he was. And he said, it's-- the only chance that we have is to escape. And the others didn't believe him. And I think that more Jews would have survived if they would have tried to escape, or if they would have tried to fight. This is why-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [DRINKS] this is why I think Israel is very important, because-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I don't know what's happening. [SOBBING] I should've done this. Sorry. This is why I think Israel is very important, because it's-- it's shown us that we can fight. Instead of just being taken to the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like sheep to the slaughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's better to fight and die, than to die like they did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think my father was very courageous to do that, and I wouldn't be sitting here now if he hadn't done that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he obviously had a lot of friends who were not Jewish. You know, I mean, like-- like I have a lot of non-Jewish friends here. And if something happened in Bellevue Hill, maybe I could go next door. I have friends and so on who, because of our previous friendship, would try and help us, you see. And I think that he had a lot of friends, and he organized the escape. Now, they may have done it for various reasons. I know that one of the reasons they did it for was for money. But that doesn't matter anyway. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At least he was able to organize it. Now they also-- a lot of them took money and spill-- spilled the beans to the-- to the Gestapo. So you have to be pretty careful whom you're going to trust. And the others helped you to escape. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were risking their lives as well, of course, you understand, so you can see it from their point of view as well, because if they were caught, they risked their lives as well. Now, some people are willing to risk things for lots of money. You've got to remember, these people were very poor, and the Jews were relatively well off. I mean, they were the businessmen and the educated people in a small village like that. And they had, you know-- they had possessions, obviously. And so you had to find the right person to-- to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took a risk, and it was easier to take the money and become an informant of the Gestapo than to take the money and try and let you escape. And if you-- and if you were taken, you know, you were-- you were also shot, but with the Jew. So there are good people amongst everybody, I'm sure. Can you remember the escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can remember. I can remember the escape very well. It was a hot day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my mother made me wear three jumpers. I couldn't understand it. Now, she didn't tell me anything, because you know what children tend to do. They tend to talk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] can we stop? I think I want to stop it for a while. It's OK. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's so stupid. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the three jumpers that your mother made you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very-- it must've been summertime. Not that it ever really gets hot in summer in Poland, but it was a very hot day and I was sweating like a pig. And my mother made me wear these three bloody jumpers over a-- and the coat, because you couldn't have this on. And I couldn't understand why she made me do this, until later when, of course, we were walking through the mountains. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened that day after you had the three jumpers put on?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto had lots of gates where people used to walk in and out. And we walked out of the gate, and there was a mail van there. The mail van in Poland in those days consisted of-- it actually had three wheels only on it. It had a cabin in the front with one wheel, and two wheels in the back and a-- and a cabin where the mail used to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a driver sitting in this thing in the front. And there was me and my mother and my father and this Adam and his parents and-- and Maricia and-- and Dusha Bilfelb. And we were laying-- and my fa-- the men were lying on the bottom, the women on top, and the children on top, because-- [LAUGHS] the weight, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how we were taken. So we-- we went into this-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I don't know why I'm crying. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have been frightened.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's terrible. I've never cried before like this. Anyway, me with my three jumpers and the coat, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we drove towards the Czech border. And in front of us was a truck, which had-- which was a big truck. It carried produce, I think. And underneath the produce were, apparently, another couple of people. And the truck was stopped somewhere about halfway along the-- the road. And we stopped before-- behind them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy walked out to have a look, and I heard rifle shots and a lot of screaming and carrying on. And then-- it lasted a while, and then we drove. And my father said to the guy in the front, what happened? And he said, in Polish, oh, they found two Jews, and they shot them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the road there, you see? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then we arrived at the border, and it was nighttime. And the guy in this-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] the guy in the van stopped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was another two guys, met us there. And as I said, it was nighttime already, and we started walking. So after lying in this little van with hardly any air for several hours, we then had to start walking. And we didn't walk very far that night. We walked to some sort of farm or other, which was next to a waterfall, because the noise of the waterfall was deafening. You couldn't hear yourself talking, and we stayed there at night. No, we stayed there for a few days. That's right, for a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us. All-- The whole group of people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were quite well fed because this was really Poland outside. This was no longer the ghetto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we had pork, [LAUGHS] but my father said it was all right. [LAUGHS] Because the farmers had a lot of pork in Poland. It was the staple diet. I don't think I liked it, but they used to fry it very well. You know? I think I did like it, but he said it was all right. And the next morning-- no, the next night-- no, a few nights later, we started walking because we only walked at nighttime, just during the night, and hid during the day. But the first place was this price which was a waterfall-- next to a waterfall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was somewhere-- I think it was the Tatra Mountains, so it was the mountain range that separates Czechoslovakia from Poland. We were on our way to-- was it Bratislava? Is that in Czechoslovakia? Bratislava. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we walked at night. This took-- it's a long way. This took about-- quite a few days, because we only walked at night and hid during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you eat that you hadn't tasted before?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had an orange she gave me once. And she was-- she was very health conscious, you see? And I think I must've been constipated because she wanted me to have-- what's that stuff that's so terrible that people give you to drink? Olive oil. Not olive oil--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cod liver.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cod liver oil, yeah. It's awfully tastes. And I said, well, I can't take this. She said, well, I'll give you half an orange if you take it. So I ate the orange, but I still didn't have the cod liver oil. I couldn't swallow it. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's the first time I ever had any oranges in my life. [LAUGHS] But I didn't have the cod liver oil.

speaker: interviewer
text: What possessions did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just the three jumpers I had. Now I realized why had the tree jumpers, because it was very cold at night. And the reason why we had to wear-- apparently, when we walked out of the ghetto, how-- how it was organized, we were only supposed to go outside and come back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you couldn't-- you know, you couldn't exactly carry a briefcase with you, or a suitcase, or look like you had a lot of clothes on. So you just-- you know, you-- you were just supposed to be strolling out-- not strolling out. I don't know where the hell we were going. But we were supposed to be going out and coming back, under some sort of guard or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how they did it. Well, no, I think I do know how, actually. You see, a lot of the guards were not Germans. They were Poles. Because the-- the Germans didn't have enough men. I mean, they were busy fighting elsewhere. In order to contain all the Jews, they had to have the help of the local population.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think a lot of them were Polish, and they were bribed, obviously. But they had the odd sprinkling of-- of Gestapo there. And it had to look sort of fairly innocuous, I suppose. And also, I suppose they had some reason why they were going out, but I don't remember. I never asked my father. I should have now. [LAUGHS] What was the color of your coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was light colored, because it was supposed to be summer. So it was a light-colored jacket. You couldn't have a-- you know, you couldn't wear an overcoat because it would look suspicious. So there were three jumpers underneath, and a light coat-- light jacket.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on your journey, what did you ask your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I didn't ask any questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my-- they said we-- yeah, I was going to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I felt a bit better. I mean, certainly once we were outside, because that's the first time I'd remembered ever being outside of a-- I suppose it's like being out of prison for the first time in your life. So I think it felt good. But it was very frightening because we were walking at night, and we were separated quite a bit. I walked with one of the guides. My parents walked somewhere else. And I got lost one night, and I couldn't scream. I'm-- oh, that's right. I was not allowed to-- to speak or scream or say anything. I was just supposed to walk. And somehow in the-- and it was pitch dark. It was as dark as hell. And the guide lost me, so I didn't know what to do. And I knew I couldn't scream out, so-- so that was out because if someone heard you, I mean, they would find you. And there were a lot of-- I suppose it was a dangerous area, I would imagine. So I just sat down and waited without saying anything. And then, I think, like-- like forever that the guide came back and picked me up. I don't know why I got lost, but I remember being lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the dark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and just sat there without saying anything. I wanted to-- to cry or scream or anything, but I-- I said-- my mother said not to say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old would you have been at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was five and a half, six, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the names of all the other people who were on your journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't re-- apart from Adam, that I just remembered-- I can't remember his parents' names. And there was Dusha. Well, actually his name was Abraham Bilfelb, and his wife, Maricia Bilfelb. He actually had a bad heard. I'm surprised that he made the journey because he always had a bad heart, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the guides?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two Pol-- Polish people. Two Polish woodsmen, I think they were. Very strong guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I liked one of them because he came and found me again. [LAUGHS] I was never so happy to see anybody, I think. But he came back for me. He could have left me behind, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you slept by day, where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in-- in the barn. [INAUDIBLE] They-- I think they must have had a wheat-growing areas, because there was a lot of wheat. They used to store it in the barn, and it was very dusty. So, in the barns, and sometimes in very small little huts. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that seemed to take forever, that journey. But it wasn't so bad, actually, apart from that one time where I got lost. But the walking I didn't mind so much, and we slept during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must have taken a couple of weeks. I think. It seemed forever, but I suppose-- but I think it took two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other incidents along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the next incident was that-- it was all prearranged. There was some sort of escape mechanism, where there was a-- people used to meet other-- you know, other people used to meet other people sort of thing, right? Because we actually came to this town, which was the first city I had ever seen. I think it was Bratislava, I think. And we were supposed to go by train there. And we arrived. Someone else picked us up there and took us to a sort of fairly modern-looking building. But it was-- it was very bad, because the sink in the bathroom, the porcelain was broken. And my mother cut her hand on it while trying to wash. And also, I had to clean my shoes that night, because I had been walking  in these shoes, and we were supposed to go on the train and look like we were citizens of the world. And we looked-- I suppose we must've looked very bedraggled, and everybody spent the night sprucing themselves up. And my mother said to me, you've got to clean your shoes so that they look very clean, because the next morning you are-- we were going on a train, and you had-- you weren't supposed to look like you'd just walked over the mountains. You were supposed to look like citizens of the-- of the city. And-- and-- and for-- I mean, we-- I didn't mention we had foreign papers. Our names were changed. That's right. And my mother said, if somebody asks you, your name is [? Mattera, ?] not Muller. Stephan [? Mattera. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that night we had to clean all our clothes and our shoes so that we would look reasonable the next day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your mother cut her hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: She cut her hand on the sink. I remember the sink. I guess they had a big hole in it. So it couldn't have been such a great place. It must have been some sort of hiding place. But it was a fairly modern-looking-- yeah, I know what it looked-- it looked different because it looked-- I had spent all that time in the barns. And this was, again, a different-- a transition. So it was-- it was just different. So it looked different and it felt different.

speaker: interviewer
text: You washed your own shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we washed them.  I had some sort of polish there, or I kept spitting on it and polishing it with something. It was-- I don't why I remember polishing these damn shoes until they really shined so I looked clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bath?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, didn't have any bath. I can't remember having any baths at all, not throughout the whole journey. I remember going to the toilet in the-- in the woods, but no baths.

speaker: interviewer
text: A toilet in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you had to go to the toilet, you had to go outside, obviously. So my mother used to take me outside into-- into the underbrush, I think. But we didn't have any showers, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other incidents along the way on this journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train journey was, again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very nerve-wracking because my parents said, you've got to be very careful. If somebody approaches you and asks you questions, right? You must just-- and they might ask you some questions, you see? Like where are you from? And you're not supposed to say you're from Bochnia, from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think I was instructed to say that I was, you know, someplace in Bratislava, or whatever the town was. And we also had to separate on the train, so I was with my mother. My father was somewhere else. The others were somewhere else so that in case it was-- you know, the train-- if somebody came up to you and you were suspicious, wouldn't-- you wouldn't all go, you see? If they had, let's say, taken my father, he wouldn't have said that his wife and son were on board. So we were all spread out over this train from different parts of the train. And it looked like we were sep-- traveling separately. Because I said, why-- why aren't we together? Because we had been, through all this period of time in such a close-- that's right-- in such a close unit. All of a sudden, I was on the train with my mother only, and the others were all over the place. And I said, well, why aren't we together?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, you've got to be very careful and do say very much. [INAUDIBLE] And there were lots of officials-looking men walking down, like you see train conductors. I never travel on trains. I don't like [INAUDIBLE] train conductors either, [LAUGHS] because they always looked like they were-- was asking you for-- for tickets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and they said, you've got to be very careful if they come up and ask you for your name or your ticket. You know I never traveled on trains? Isn't that strange? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, so [LAUGHS] I didn't-- so we-- this had to be-- it was very-- it was very worrying, and I remember being very nervous because my mother instilled this in me, how serious the situation was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought, well, here we are in heaven already. Everything's OK. But she said, no, we've got to be very careful. And we had to be-- it was important not to say too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the other people on the train, the other passengers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember any of them. She said, look out the window and just look at the scenery and try not to, sort of, run around. I was a very obedient child. I must have been, because I never ran out. I just sat there looking out the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see out the window?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember what I saw, but I remember-- the next scene, I remember very well. I was arriving in Budapest, and there was a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] is a-- is, at the station, is a-- instead of a cab, they used to have these horse drawn carriages. Not carriages, but, you know, like they have in Central Park, in New York, where people go for-- but that used to be the mode of transport. And the other thing I don't like about trains is the platform. You know how they-- you know how the train has to come up to a certain platform, right, and it's got to be higher. And when the train's not there, you just see the platform.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've never liked that platform-looking thing because there was always-- there was always this thing about trains and deportation and-- and escape in the train. And I never liked that-- that-- the view. Do you know what I mean? When there's-- it's just a platform. You see lots of pictures of platforms with no trains. When the train is there, it looks OK because it looks like it's running into each other. When there's no train, only the platform, this looks like you're waiting for something. Screwed up life, eh? [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I remember the platform in-- in-- in Brat-- in Czechoslovakia, waiting for the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm going back. Yes, I remember the-- being very scared, and this is why I don't like that platform. But the next thing is-- is-- is this horse drawn carriage. And Budapest in 1943 was a fantastic-looking town, the first city I'd-- apart from Czechoslovakia, which wasn't all that great. But this was beautiful. I don't know, have you ever been to-- it's fantastic. It really is a very beautiful city. It was in '43, anyway. It's got the river and it had streets and houses, and-- and there was no one-- there was no-- it wasn't occupied yet. It was free. We didn't stay there very long. We stayed-- Budapest is divided into two, Buda on one side of the Danube and Pest on the other. I think we stayed in Pest, in a block of units, for a very short time. And then we went to a little village outside of the main city, a wine-growing area. That's where I-- I remember a lot about because I-- we spent a long-- quite a long time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you would have arrived in Budapest? What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it must have been summertime, again, because-- by this time, I didn't have to wear the three jumpers. And it was-- it was quite warm. It was a warm day, so it must have been summer. Yeah, it was summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was special about arriving in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the first city I have ever seen, which was-- the first metropolis, actually, the first free city where actually there was no-- there was no soldiers on the road. There were no guns. There were-- apart from police, I suppose there were. But it looked-- you know, it just looked like something that I had never seen, so it was just fantastic. And actually going in this horse drawn carriage, you know, was just amazing, along the cobblestone streets. And it was just really quite something. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the reactions of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think they seemed-- they seemed to be smiling and happy as if they-- you know, I think because they realized they escaped. Even if it proved to be short lived, at least they felt that they had escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the village that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my Hungarian is no longer very good, but I think it was called [HUNGARIAN]. But I don't know how to spell it, and most of my Hungarian friends don't seem to know-- know it. It must've been a very small. It was a very small town. It had one central road through it. And the house that we stayed in, on the farm we stayed in, didn't have any floor boards. It was only the bare earth. So it was most-- must've been a pretty primitive town.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not sure why we chose that. I think-- I think there was some sort of underground in Hungary which used to say, well-- because we were supposed to be Polish, right? As long as you were scattered all over the-- you didn't stay in one central area, you had a better chance. So they said, you go there or go there or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we finished up going to this place. There were a few other Jews there. Not very many. It was mostly the Hungarian-- Hungarian peasants. Now, Hungary again, was very-- after this initial euphoria, I was told that they-- Hungary was also a very dangerous country because there was the Hungarian police.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been this sort of imminent-- they must've known that the Germans were coming in. I mean, they obviously didn't fight, right? And I suppose they were like fifth [? columnists ?] there. They sort of went in there first to-- to-- not that I'm a historian-- but they must have gone into a country and sort of softened them up a little bit. So there were a lot of infiltrators in there. And they had infiltrated the Hungarian police, or they had some-- a lot of men who were really very vicious, because they wanted to impress their masters. And therefore, they were even worse than some of the Germans, because of the fact that they went-- there was sort of an overkill. And this is why it wasn't good to stay in the main cities. It was better to be out in the country where they were less likely to be than they were in the metropolis-- metro-- metropolitan area. You know? And this is why we went to a very small village. Change of tape now. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape two of the interview with Stephen Muller. It's the 29th of September, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
